replies.msg: The English Channel (French: la Manche, "The Sleeve"; German: Ärmelkanal, "Sleeve Channel"; Breton: Mor Breizh, "Sea of Brittany"; Cornish: Mor Bretannek, "British Sea"), also called simply the Channel, is the body of water that separates southern England from northern France, and links the southern part of the North Sea to the Atlantic Ocean. It is the busiest shipping area in the world.

replies.msg:
.... Hard to say if honest or fake
"County" was a joke.
[seriou]
The corresponding regional administrative division for province in USA is state, as in a federation. The official name of a federated state can be "Commonwealth", found in some of the original 13 Colonies.
It is sometimes argued the EU is a federation. If this becomes the reality (like some fantasy story or futuristic sci-fi setting), the constituent member sovereign states would become federated, and they would take on this other meaning of "state".
In itself, a "state" is the product of what a government controls geographically.
Canada and Mexico use province, and I think New Mexico was a province under Mexican/Spanish rule.

Well actually there are a lot of English tutorial podcasts with different accents. The one I am listening to is Luke’s English podcast, found it on Spotify, sort of an easy one, designed for Europeans who wanna learn Brit English such as accent and useful phrase and stuffs by talking about daily topics

replies.msg:
Also it's depend on the use of the English as well, for instance, news article website like BBC, New York Times or Atlantic Monthly helps enhancing your critical thinking, and be able to express yourself in a more complex way, but it won't help improving your English conversation in daily life, I mean at least you aren't going to use those vocabularies found on the articles correct?

Keep it in mind, English is one of those interesting languages which enable you to create abstraction to express your thought and idea in certain direction, but in general you don't need to know as much vocabs as possible in order to maintain regular conversation. Just using some simple sentence structures found on forums are suffice.

replies.msg: OP’s mom was buying groceries when several 180cm tall young men with 30cm long dicks who scored 30 on the HKCEE dragged her into a disabled toilet stall. All of them proceeded to take of their pants and said, “Hey OP’s mom, sorry to bother you! You really want it, don’t you?” They then spread OP’s mom’s legs, and they just rammed into her. The whole toilet was filled with flopping sounds. Each one of them finished their job one after the other, while politely saying “excuse me” to OP’s mom beforehand. By the thirtieth guy, OP’s mom said: “Although this feels amazing, what are you doing this for?” And the thirtieth guy said, “Sorry, auntie, we really didn’t want to do this, but your son said he found awesome content on HKGolden and there wasn’t any when I clicked into the thread. We all pressed F5 dozens of times until we just came here to take a ticket and queue. I’ve gotta go now, I have to go to work right after I finish this.” OP’s mom asked, “How many people are queueing right now?” The young man replied, “Ask the next one.” Flop flop flop… When the hundredth guy came, OP’s mom asked again, “How many people are queueing right now?” The hundredth guy replied, “I have no idea, there were many people who came on the MTR with me, and when the train doors opened, everyone rushed to here just like what happens in a book fair. I hear the queue’s at two thousand people right now. I’ll talk to you later, I’m gonna start.” Flop flop flop flop…
